narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like a solid LSAT score and coming from a high family income bracket, which put you on par with or slightly ahead of many applicants in those areas. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 is right around the average for students in this dataset, and planning to attend full-time aligns with the vast majority who do so. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 49.7%—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed on that critical test. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical 5.53 average, signaling to the model that your later academic performance raised concerns about bar exam readiness. This outweighed positives like your undergraduate GPA, which helped push toward a passing prediction by matching typical strong performers, and your LSAT score of 37, also a favorable nudge since it's slightly above average. Your Year 1 decile of 3, while low compared to the average 5.71, played a secondary role in heightening the risk, though less than your later grades. Meanwhile, your high family income and full-time plans offered smaller boosts toward success. Compared to typical students who pass, your mid-tier undergrad record and test score were competitive, but the dip in school deciles stood out as a red flag against those who maintain higher rankings throughout. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—it's the model weighing patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT or GPA-enhancing prep if possible, and reapplying with updated credentials that address these gaps. Many have turned similar profiles around; you're capable of that too. Keep pushing—law needs voices like yours.
